skill.name: code-patterns-practices
skill.description: React Native coding patterns, best practices, and common solutions for mobile development. Use when implementing features or refactoring code.
skill.summary: Apply practical React Native patterns for components, state, data fetching, performance, and error handling.
skill.author: BabakBar

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found command execution, script, network, weak crypto, and reconnaissance patterns, but review shows they are false positives in Markdown documentation and React Native examples. The skill allows only Read, Write, and Edit tools and contains no prompt injection, executable helper scripts, credential access, or data exfiltration logic. It is safe to publish with low residual risk from benign code examples that mention fetch and dynamic imports.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static alerts for Ruby or shell backtick execution correspond to Markdown TypeScript code fence delimiters and example snippets. No executable shell command, subprocess call, or user input command path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are Markdown code fence delimiters around React Native and TypeScript examples. The skill metadata permits only Read, Write, and Edit tools, so these examples are not directly executed by the skill.

low_findings.title: Benign React Examples Misclassified as Script or Network Behavior
low_findings.description: The dynamic import and fetch call are illustrative React patterns inside Markdown. The fetch target is a relative application API path, and the import target is a local component name.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both patterns are located inside documentation examples and do not include credential access, external destinations, or obfuscation. They demonstrate normal React Native data fetching and lazy loading.

low_findings.title: Resource Links and Headings Misclassified as Dangerous Patterns
low_findings.description: The weak crypto and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from descriptive text and headings. The hardcoded URLs are public documentation resources, not outbound exfiltration endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain skill description text, an anti-patterns heading, and resource links to React documentation sites. No cryptographic operation, system command, or suspicious remote collection behavior appears there.

content.value_statement: React Native teams need consistent patterns for components, state, data fetching, performance, and errors. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward practical implementation choices.
actual_capabilities: Explains custom hooks, compound components, render props, and context usage.
Suggests local, shared, and Zustand state management patterns.
Shows data fetching with async effects, cleanup, loading state, and error state.
Guides memoization, stable callbacks, component memoization, and lazy loading decisions.
Covers React Native safe area and keyboard avoiding patterns.
Highlights common anti-patterns such as massive components and direct state mutation.
limitations: It is a concise pattern guide, not a full React Native architecture manual.
It does not include automated lint rules, tests, or runnable project templates.
Examples use simplified code and may need adaptation for specific app frameworks.
It does not cover native module development, release engineering, or platform store setup.

use_cases.title: Implement a Mobile Feature
use_cases.description: Use the guide to choose component, state, fetching, and error patterns before writing a new screen.

use_cases.title: Refactor Existing Components
use_cases.description: Apply smaller components, custom hooks, and shared state patterns to reduce complexity in a mature codebase.

use_cases.title: Review Code Quality
use_cases.description: Compare pull requests against the listed best practices and anti-patterns for maintainable mobile code.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner developer selecting an implementation approach
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-patterns-practices skill to recommend a React Native pattern for this feature. Explain when to use it and what tradeoffs to watch.

prompt_templates.title: Improve a Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-patterns-practices skill to review this React Native component. Suggest how to split responsibilities, extract hooks, and improve state handling.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Data Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Team deciding between local, context, and global state
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-patterns-practices skill to design the state and data fetching approach for this screen. Include loading, error, cleanup, and retry considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture Choices
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before scaling a mobile feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the code-patterns-practices skill to audit this React Native feature plan. Identify pattern mismatches, performance risks, error handling gaps, and mobile-specific issues.

output_examples.input: Review a profile screen that fetches user data and manages modal state.
output: Use a custom hook for profile fetching and cleanup.
Keep modal state local unless other screens need it.
Return explicit loading and error states to the screen component.
Add an error boundary only around failure-prone child sections.

output_examples.input: Choose state management for authentication data and theme settings.
output: Use global state for the authenticated user because many screens need it.
Use context for theme because it is a cross-cutting UI concern.
Avoid prop drilling through navigation layers.
Keep transient form state local to each screen.

output_examples.input: Find risks in a proposed React Native refactor.
output: Large components should be split by responsibility.
Effects need cleanup to avoid stale updates.
Keyboard and safe area behavior need mobile-specific review.

best_practices: Start with local state, then move to context or global state when sharing is required.
Extract reusable behavior into custom hooks and keep components focused on rendering.
Handle loading, error, cleanup, safe area, and keyboard behavior as first-class requirements.
anti_patterns: Using global state for every value before proving it is shared across screens.
Adding memoization everywhere without measuring a real performance issue.
Leaving data fetching effects without cancellation or stale update protection.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps choose and apply React Native patterns for components, state, fetching, performance, errors, and mobile layout concerns.

faq.question: Does it generate complete applications?
faq.answer: No. It provides focused guidance and examples that an assistant can apply inside an existing app or feature plan.

faq.question: Can it help with TypeScript code?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples use TypeScript-style React patterns and can guide typed component and hook design.

faq.question: Does it require shell or network tools?
faq.answer: No. The skill declares Read, Write, and Edit tools only. Network examples are documentation snippets, not skill operations.

faq.question: Is it useful for code reviews?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists best practices and anti-patterns that help review React Native pull requests.

faq.question: What topics are outside its scope?
faq.answer: It does not cover native module development, deployment pipelines, app store releases, or comprehensive testing strategy.
